abbreviations.FolN: Foliar nitrogen content (in % by weight) for the leaves of the species.
abbreviations.SLWmax: Specific leaf weight at the top of the canopy.
abbreviations.SLWDel: Rate of change in the weight of the leaves from the top of the canopy (SLWmax) to each sublayer under the canopy.
abbreviations.MaxLAI: Maximum leaf area index (LAI) that a species can achieve in its lifetime (for an age cohort ?).
abbreviations.TOFol: Fraction of foliage biomass lost per year.
abbreviations.TORoot: Fraction of root biomass lost per year.
abbreviations.TOWood: Fraction of wood biomass lost per year.
abbreviations.AmaxA: Intercept of the relationship between foliar N and maximum net photosynthetic rate.
abbreviations.AmaxB: Slope of the relationship betwee foliar N and maximum net photosynthetic rate.
abbreviations.HalfSat: Half saturation light level for photosynthesis.
abbreviations.H1: Water stress parameter according to Feddes et al. (1978) (Fig. 59).
abbreviations.H2: Water stress parameter according to Feddes et al. (1978) (Fig. 59).
abbreviations.H3: Water stress parameter according to Feddes et al. (1978) (Fig. 59).
abbreviations.H4: Water stress parameter according to Feddes et al. (1978) (Fig. 59).
abbreviations.PsnAgeRed: Reduction factor of photosynthesis as the age of an age cohort increases.
abbreviations.LeafOnMinT: Minimum temperature for activate growing season.
abbreviations.PsnTMin: Minimum average daytime temperature for photosynthesis.
abbreviations.PsnTOpt: Optimal average daytime temperature for photosynthesis.
abbreviations.PsnTMax: Maximum average daytime temperature for photosynthesis.
abbreviations.ColTol: Cold tolerance (coldest temperature that the species can survive).
abbreviations.k (parameter): Canopy light attenuation constant (light extinction coefficient).
abbreviations.DNSC: Proportion of Non Structural (ressources) carbon from the total activate biomass of the cohort that will be maintained as long as net photosynthesis exceeds maintenance respiration.
abbreviations.FracBelowG: Fraction of non-foliar biomass that is allocated belowground (root pool).
abbreviations.EstMoist: Scaling parameter that changes the maximum expected value of fWater when computing the probability of establishment.
abbreviations.EstRad: Scaling parameter that changes the maximum expected value of fRad when computing the probability of establishment.
abbreviations.FracFol: Fraction of the active woody biomass (above or belowground) of a cohort that determines the amount of foliage maintained each year.
abbreviations.FrActWd: Shape parameter of the negative exponential function that calculates the amount of woody biomass that has active xylem capable of supporting foliage.
abbreviations.CO2HalfSatEff: Slope coefficient used to reduce HalfSat (= increase shade tolerance) as CO2 concentration increase.
abbreviations.CO2AMaxBEff: Proportional change in AmaxB at 550ppm CO2 relative to 350ppm.
abbreviations.O3StomataSens: Species susceptibility of the stomata to ozone-induced sluggishness.
abbreviations.O3GrowthSens: Species susceptibility to ozone-induced tissue damage.
abbreviations.MaxFolN: Controls the dynamic response of foliar nitrogen (FolN) to light.
abbreviations.FolNShape: Controls the dynamic response of foliar nitrogen (FolN) to light.
abbreviations.MaxFracFol: Controls the dynamic response of the fraction of Foliage (FracFol) to light.
abbreviations.FracFolShape: Controls the dynamic response of the fraction of Foliage (FracFol) to light.
abbreviations.LifeForm: Specifies the life form (stature) of the species to determine the limits to the species major canopy layer.
abbreviations.MossScalar: Unit converter from biomass to depth in the case of a pseudo species (moss).
abbreviations.MaxCanopyLayer: Caps the number of canopy layers implemented in the model.
abbreviations.LayerThreshRatio: Define how age cohorts of a given species are grouped together into canopy layers. Correspond to the minimum ratio of cohort woody biomass density between two cohorts that will place them in different canopy layers.
abbreviations.PARunits: Indicates the units for PAR and HalfSat values. See Climate Input File for PAR and Species Parameters File for HalfSat.
abbreviations.IMAX: Defines the degree of subdivision into different canopy sublayers for each age cohort.
abbreviations.DVPD1: Coefficient for converting the vapor pressure deficit (VPD) into a reduction factor for photosynthesis caused by water pressure (fDVPD).
abbreviations.DVPD2: Coefficient for converting the vapor pressure deficit (VPD) into a reduction factor for photosynthesis caused by water pressure (fDVPD).
abbreviations.BFolResp: Define the Base Foliar Respiration Fraction (the foliar respiration as a fraction of the maximum photosynthetic rate).
abbreviations.MaintResp: Represents the loss of Non Structural Carbon (NSC) due to maintenance respiration as a fraction of the active wood biomass of the cohort.
abbreviations.Q10: Coefficient of the Q10 relationship to compute the foliar respiration.
abbreviations.FolLignin: Mass fraction of lignin in foliage tissue.
abbreviations.KWdLit: Annual decomposition rate of woody litter (decay constant k in equations).
abbreviations.InitialNSC: Amount of Non Structural Carbon assigned to newly established cohorts.
abbreviations.CFracBiomass: Carbon fraction of biomass by weight in an age cohort.
abbreviations.PrecipEvents: Number of precipitation events during a month.
abbreviations.PrecipEventsWithReplacement: Defines the logic of assignation of the precipitation from a precipitation event to the canopy sub layers.
abbreviations.ETExtCoeff: Canopy extinction coefficient to reduce soil water evaporation as LAI increases (because of shadow created by the canopy ?).
abbreviations.RETCropCoeff: Coefficient that scales the reference evapotranspiration (RET).
abbreviations.PreventEstablishment: Turns species establishment on new cells on or off.
abbreviations.Wythers: Turns the Wythers correction on or off.
abbreviations.DTEMP: Turns the PnET-II DTEMP temperature reduction factor on or off.
abbreviations.MaxPest: Tuning parameter to raise or lower the establishment probability of species by a same proportion.
abbreviations.AmaxFrac: Fraction of the early morning instantaneous photosynthetic rate that represents the mean daily photosynthetic rate.
abbreviations.InvertPest: Activates the optional inversion of the effect of fRad on the probability establishment as a function of HalfSat.
abbreviations.SoilIceDepth: Activates the optional simulation of soil ice depth and its effects on water leakage.
abbreviations.LeakageFrostDepth: Soil ice depth at which frozen soil no longer impedes soil drainage.
abbreviations.FrostFactor: Tuning parameter to adjust soil ice depth.
abbreviations.InitialCommunitiesSpinup: Specifies how the biomass of the initial age cohorts/communities in each cells are generated.
abbreviations.SpinupWaterStress: Activates waterlogging stress during spin up procedures.
abbreviations.Parallel: Specifies the number of CPU threads used for parallel execution.
abbreviations.CohortStacking: Specifies the canopy cohort processing method.
abbreviations.CanopySumScale: Scales the degree to which cumulativve canopy proportions cannot exceed 1.

children.value: Finding these initial values for your tree species might be the most work you will have to do here, especially for the “Core species parameters” (see below). Since I’ve made these notebooks with the goal of calibrating as many canadian tree species as possible, you’ll find sources of data and functions to compute some parameters for canadian tree species. But if you’re in the US or in Europe, or in another part of the world where previous LANDIS-II studies are scarce, you will have to to some digging.
**At the end of this notebook, you should have three

files containing your parameters for each species, in the format used by the rest of the functions in these notebooks : one for the core species parameters, one for the PnET species parameters, and one for the PnET generic parameters (see below for explanations about the terms). These three
files should be in the folder
Remember that as much as possible, it is best to get parameters values for your species in your study area, or near your study area, to avoid confusion with local variants or phenotypes. See
Some important notions before we start

Of course, some parameters (especially some core species parameters which represent global life history traits) don’t need to be local; and for other, you will have a lot of difficulty finding more local estimates. It’s alright, and it’s part of the complexity in calibrating a complex model such as LANDIS-II and PnET-Succession. Just take the best values you find, note the limitations and potential implications or your choices on what the model will produce, and carry on !

Change in core species parameters between LANDIS-II v7 and LANDIS-II v8

The v7 of LANDIS-II has been used by a lot of studies in North America; but with the recent (2025-2026) switch to the version 8 of the model, the core species parameters have been changed : shade tolerance and fire tolerance have been removed, the vegetative reproduction is no longer a “yes/no” but a probability and you must indicate the sprouting ages, etc.
So if you take a look at previous studies with LANDIS-II, you will most likely find the old format of parameters, and you might need to do a bit more research to find the new ones.
What do I do if I can’t find published parameters for a given tree species ? Where do I find initial parameter values ?

This is the though part. The big problem with the PnET models (as explanation in the user guide) is that they are described as models easy to parameterize because you can just use empirical values as parameters; but strangely, it’s extremely hard to find empirical values for the parameters used in the model, even just the most important ones !
So you might find yourself without any good source of parameters for a species you want to calibrate. If you find yourself in this kind of situation,
there is a section at the end of this notebook that will allow you to generate the parameters for a tree species based on its shade tolerance score (from 1 to 5) and its evergreen/deciduous type
. This is done using the advice of

children.title: Former researcher (now retired) at the US Forest Service. Eric was largely responding for develloping the PnET-Succession extension of LANDIS-II.

children.value: in the PnET-Succession user guide; it’s far from perfect, but it should produce a parameter set that makes sense.

However, you will still need estimates of the growth curve in ideal conditions (see next notebook) and longevity data to be able to calibrate your species. And if you can find parameters or empirical values for the parameters in the litterature, you should try to do so.

children.value: In PnET-Succession, 4 parameters are related to the effect of water in the soils on tree growth :

children.value: These parameters together define a curve relating the pressure head in the soil with the value of fWater, which is the photosythesis reduction factor linked to water. Here is an example from the user guide :

The idea of the trapezoid is that the photosynthesis of tree is overall very little affect by the amount of water present in the soil (fWater = 1, no effect on the photosynthesis); unless we arrive at thresholds of waterlogging or drought (pressure head too small or too high) where the photosynthesis will abruptly diminish. The user guide even recommends to use a value of

children.title: Water stress parameter according to Feddes et al. (1978) (Fig. 59).
Water stress parameter according to Feddes et al. (1978) (Fig. 59).

children.value: (the parameters related to high values of pressure head = drought conditions) to be the same, so that photosynthesis simply stops in drought months.
Be careful about the signs (positive or negative) used for the pressure head ! I’ve had a lot of confusion about this.

The pressure head is a measure of water pression in the soil. It represents the following idea : If I put a cylinder in contact with the soil, will it be filled with water because there is so much water in the soil that it wants to get out (pressure, which happens during waterlogging); or will the soil instead “suck” water because it’s lacking water (suction, which happens during droughts) ?

The problem is that normally, a suction normally results in negative values of pressure head (if I had water in my cylinder, the water would go down in the soils); while pressure results in positive values (if the water pushes up into the cylinder because of pressure, how much does it pushes up ?). Pressure head is therefore the measurement, in units of distance, of how up or down the water goes when put in contact with the soil.

But in PnET-Succession, the sign convention is reversed by using the “absolute” values of pressure head. What that means is that in the case of PnET-Succession, the higher the pressure head, the more we are in drought; and vice-versa. As such, the drought parameters

children.title: Water stress parameter according to Feddes et al. (1978) (Fig. 59).
Water stress parameter according to Feddes et al. (1978) (Fig. 59).

children.value: often have positive values above 100; while the waterlogging parameters

children.title: Water stress parameter according to Feddes et al. (1978) (Fig. 59).
Water stress parameter according to Feddes et al. (1978) (Fig. 59).

children.value: are often near 0, or even negative. Keep that in mind !
We sadly lack empirical data to get good estimates of these 4 parameters, and even more for many species. Although
tried to use empirical/experimental data to derive values of

children.value: As such, ideally, we would calibrate this parameters to ensure that the tree species react in the right way on the right soils in the landscape.
Sadly, I haven’t found a way to do this calibration properly, and I’m lacking time. As such, I went with a simpler option :
We can simply create a regression (linear or non-linear) of the score of Drought and Waterlogging tolerance acquired from
and previously published parameters (i.e. those the
which have been derived from an empiical experiment. This is quick and easy, and will make sure that we use parameters similar to previous studies.

The default of this approach is that we don’t do any calibration, and just re-use what have been done before. It’s also deeply reliant on the hypothesis that these parameters are completly dependant on a measure as fuzzy as the “drought tolerance” or “waterlogging tolerance” of a give tree species. It also will not produce parameters that are in any way contextual to our landscape (soils, precipitations, water in the soils); we just have to hope that the model will produce soil water content that make sense for our landscape (although this can be explored).
Still, I believe that this will do for now.

As my time is limited, I went with a simple approach of statistical regression based on already published values, and a new dataset of species temperature ranges published by Morgane Dendoncker from theme 1 in DIVERSE for many species in north america. See
By exploring the data, I noticed some strong correlations between some variables of

and previously published values for these parameters. The causes of these correlation is quite obvious; for example, the fact that there is an important positive correlation between the 5th percentile of the average annual temperatures of where a tree species is located (one of the variables from

children.title: Minimum average daytime temperature for photosynthesis.

children.value: is explained by the fact that at the northern edge of the species distribution (i.e. the 5th percentile of the temperatures in its range), the temperature becomes so low that the number of Growing Degrees Days (GDD) becomes close to what the species can survive, which implies that the average annual temperature becomes close to the minimum temperature for growth for the species.

). However, most of the approach relies on the values used by the training (here, the parameters of

In the end, I expect this simple approach to be quite robust, and to produce parameters that will make for some quite realistic growing seasons; but a more localized calibration based on a new methodology is certaintly possible. See Notebook 100 for a discussion on this.

for species in resultDict:
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["PsnTMin"] = str(round(resultDict[species]["PsnTMin"],2))
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["PsnTOpt"] = str(round(resultDict[species]["PsnTOpt"],2))
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["PsnTMax"] = str(round(resultDict[species]["PsnTMax"],2))
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species]["LeafOnMinT"] = str(round(resultDict[species]["LeafOnMinT"],2))

with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/InitialGenericParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters, f)

children.label: Generating a full species parameter set for PnET-Succession using shade/drought/waterlogging tolerance, temperature information, and evergreen/deciduous status

children.value: It might be that you find yourself without initial values for a given species that you want to calibrate; maybe because this tree species has never been calibrated/parameterized in PnET-Succession of in the PnET models at large, or maybe there are a lot of parameters for which you don’t have initial values.
The following code cell is made to generate a full species parameter set for several species for which you have a full latin name. This is done by using the calibration tips of

children.title: Former researcher (now retired) at the US Forest Service. Eric was largely responding for develloping the PnET-Succession extension of LANDIS-II.

children.value: . In it, Eric proposes initial parameters values dependant on wherever your species in a deciduous or evergreen tree, and its shade tolerance. It’s far from ideal; but seeing that Eric is one of the most knowledgeable experts on the model, I think it’s a good start.

The drought and temperature tolerance parameters will be derived using the same methodology as the previous code sections. For the rest of the parameters, the rules with which they will be derived can eb found in the
. The evergreen/deciduous type and the shade tolerance for the species is derived from the dataset of
If your tree species cannot be found in the dataset, then the function will not work
; you will have to provide your own shade tolerance and evergreen/deciduous classification. The shade tolerance must range from 1 (very intolerant to shade) to 5 (very tolerant).
To use the code cell, simply provide the code and full latin name of the species for which you want to generate a full species parameter set at the beginning of the code
If you indicate species for which you have already indicated parameters above, the values of their parameters will be replaced !
Simply make sure that the species you indicate here don’t have parameters already indicated in the tables of the previous sections, or the following code cell will simply replace these values.
This code cell only generate PnET-Succession species parameters; you might still need to provide core species parameters !

The core species parameters of LANDIS-II (longevity, effective dispersal distance, vegetative reproduction probability, etc.) are not dealt with by the following code cell. This is because these parameter (which correspond to life history traits of the species) are more difficult to find, and you cannot predict them using the simple rules proposed by

children.title: Former researcher (now retired) at the US Forest Service. Eric was largely responding for develloping the PnET-Succession extension of LANDIS-II.

children.value: To facilitate our calibration of tree species in Canada, I have compiled published values of these parameters for as many canadian species as possible and tried to find these values using the sources often used in LANDIS-II studies for these species. The code will therefore retrieve these and add them to the .json file containing the initial core species parameters values.

If one of the species you want to calibrate isn’t found in the parameters I have compiled, you will get an error message saying that you need to find these parameters by yourself. See the section about parameter sources above for some potential sources to help you find them. If you have no source, you will surely require expert opinion. Once you have obtained them, indicate these core species parameters in the corresponding table in the previous sections. You don’t need to add these species to the PnET-Succession species parameter table that comes afterwards, as these parameters will be obtained by the following code cell.

from functionsForCalibration import *
import json
import pandas as pd
import os


# Load dictionnary of additional species codes/full latin names
# INDICATE THE NAME OF THE SPECIES FOR WHICH YOU WANT AN ADDITIONAL PARAMETER SET HERE !
additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary = {
    "ACER.SAH":"Acer saccharum",
    "LARI.LAR":"Larix laricina",
    "POPU.TRE":"Populus tremuloides"
}

niinemetsValladaresData = pd.read_csv("./ReferencesAndData/Others/NiinemetsEtValladares_AppendixTable_full.csv")

dendonckerEtAlData = pd.read_csv("./ReferencesAndData/Others/DendonckerEtAl2026_Average_niche_all_spp.csv")

resultDict = {}

# For each species,
for speciesName in additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary:
    print(f"Getting parameters for {speciesName}")
    resultDict[speciesName] = dict()
    
    # Get shade tolerance/Drought tolerance and tree/wood type from niinemets and al.
    # If the species is not found in Niinemets and Valladares, we ask for a manual input.
    if additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName] not in list(niinemetsValladaresData["Species"]):
        print("Species " + str(additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]) + " is not found in the data of Niinemets and Valladares (2006). Please enter the information of the species manually :")
        shade_tolerance = -999
        while shade_tolerance < 1 or shade_tolerance > 5:
            shade_tolerance = float(input("Please input the shade tolerance of " + str(additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]) + " (from 1 = very intolerant to 5 = very tolerant"))
        tree_type = "none"
        while tree_type != "evergreen" and tree_type != "deciduous":
            tree_type = input("Please input the tree type (evergreen or deciduous) of " + str(additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]) + " :")
        wood_type = "none"
        while wood_type != "hardwood" and tree_type != "softwood":
            wood_type = input("Please input the wood type type (hardwood or softwood) of " + str(additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]) + " :")
    # If the species is in Niinemets and Valladares, we get the values from the dataset.
    else:
        rowWithSpecies = niinemetsValladaresData[niinemetsValladaresData["Species"] == additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]]
        # Some values in the dataset of Niinemets and Valladares are inferior to 1 or superior to 5;
        # not sure why. Not explained in the article. We deal with these situations by cropping the values to 1-5.
        shade_tolerance = min(5 , max(1, list(rowWithSpecies["Shade tolerance"])[0]))
        if list(rowWithSpecies["Evergreen"])[0] == "Y":
            tree_type = "evergreen"
        else:
            tree_type = "deciduous"
        if list(rowWithSpecies["Gymnosperm"])[0] == "Y":
            wood_type = "softwood"
        else:
            wood_type = "hardwood"

    # This function uses the rules indicated by Eric Gustafson in the user guide of PnET-Succession v5.1.
    # There is two parameters from which Gustafson has no rule to get them : TOFol and FrActWd.
    # For these, I used the averaged values for deciduous/evergreen and hardwood/softwoods in previous publications of PnET-Succession parameters
    # this gives the following :
    # - TOFol : 1 for deciduous trees, 0.3 for evergreens
    # - FrActWd : 0.000035 for hardwoods, 0.000029 for softwoods
    resultDict[speciesName].update(get_parameters_gustafson_rules(shade_tolerance, tree_type, wood_type))


    ############################################################
    # Get the values of H1/H2/H3/H4
    # Getting drought and wl tolerance from NiinemetsAndValladares
    # Again, if the species is not in the niinemets and valladares dataset, we ask for the data
    if additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName] not in list(niinemetsValladaresData["Species"]):
        print("Species " + str(additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]) + " is not found in the data of Niinemets and Valladares (2006). Please enter the information of the species manually :")
        drought_tolerance = -999
        while drought_tolerance < 1 or drought_tolerance > 5:
            drought_tolerance = float(input("Please input the drought tolerance of " + str(additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]) + " (from 1 = very intolerant to 5 = very tolerant"))
        waterlogging_tolerance = -999
        while waterlogging_tolerance < 1 or waterlogging_tolerance > 5:
            waterlogging_tolerance = float(input("Please input the waterlogging tolerance of " + str(additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]) + " (from 1 = very intolerant to 5 = very tolerant"))

    # If the species is in Niinemets and Valladares, we get the values from the dataset.
    else:
        rowWithSpecies = niinemetsValladaresData[niinemetsValladaresData["Species"] == additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]]
        drought_tolerance = min(5 , max(1, list(rowWithSpecies["Drought tolerance"])[0]))
        waterlogging_tolerance = min(5 , max(1, list(rowWithSpecies["Waterlogging tolerance"])[0]))
    
    resultDict[speciesName].update(predict_H_parameters(
    drought_tolerance=drought_tolerance,
    waterlogging_tolerance=waterlogging_tolerance,
    plot=False,
    gam_summary=False
    ))
    
    # Getting the values for the temperature parameters
    # if the species is not in the Dendoncker et al. dataset, we ask for the data
    if additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName] not in list(dendonckerEtAlData["Species"]):
        print("Species " + str(additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]) + " is not found in the data of Dendoncker et al. (2026). Please enter the information of the species manually :")
        MAT_q05 = -9999
        while MAT_q05 < -273:
            MAT_q05 = float(input("Please input MAT_q05 (5th percentile of the Mean Annual Temperature for the entire spatial distribution range of the species " + str(additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]) + ", i.e. the lowest annual temperatures in places where it survives) :"))
        
        MAT_q95 = -9999
        while MAT_q95 < -273:
            MAT_q95 = float(input("Please input MAT_q95 (95th percentile of the Mean Annual Temperature for the entire spatial distribution range of the species " + str(additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]) + ", i.e. the highest annual temperatures in places where it survives) :"))
        
        MTWM_q95 = -9999
        while MTWM_q95 < -273:
            MTWM_q95 = float(input("Please input MTWM_q95 (95th percentile of the Mean temperature of the Warmest Month for the entire spatial distribution range of the species " + str(additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]) + ", i.e. the highest mean temperature of the warmest month in places where it survives) :"))
        
    # If the species is in the dataset, we get the values from the dataset.
    else:
        rowWithSpecies = dendonckerEtAlData[dendonckerEtAlData["Species"] == additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName]]
        MAT_q05 = list(rowWithSpecies["MAT.q05"])[0]
        MAT_q95 = list(rowWithSpecies["MAT.q95"])[0]
        MTWM_q95 = list(rowWithSpecies["MTWM.q95"])[0]
    
    resultDict[speciesName].update(predict_temperature_parameters_single_species(
                        species_code = speciesName,
                        species_latin_name = additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary[speciesName],
                        MAT_q05 = MAT_q05,
                        MAT_q95 = MAT_q95,
                        MTWM_q95 = MTWM_q95,
                        return_df = False,
                        plotAndSummaries = False)[0][speciesName])

    
    print(resultDict[speciesName])
    print("\n")


# Export the new species to the existing .json files
dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json'))
# In case no initial PnET Species parameters exist
if os.path.exists('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json'):
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json'))
else:
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters = dict()
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["LandisData"] = "PnETSpeciesParameters"
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"] = dict()
dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters = json.load(open('./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/InitialGenericParameters.json'))

for species in resultDict:
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species] = dict()
    for parameter in resultDict[species]:
        dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species][parameter] = str(resultDict[species][parameter])

for species in dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"]:
    # We put the parameters in the same order for all species
    dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species] = {k: dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species][k] for k in sorted(dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters["PnETSpeciesParameters"][species])}

with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialCoreSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialCoreSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETSpeciesParameters, f)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/InitialGenericParameters.json", "w") as f: json.dump(dictOfInitialPnETGenericParameters, f)

# At the end : add the additional species codes/full latin names to the existing species dictionnary name
with open('./SpeciesParametersSets/speciesNamesDictionnary.json', 'r') as file: speciesDict = json.load(file)
speciesDict.update(additionalSpeciesNamesDictionnary)
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/speciesNamesDictionnary.json", "w") as f: json.dump(speciesDict, f)
